content.text: are ES2017 keywords that allow asynchronous code to be written in a style that reads like synchronous code. Under the hood they are syntactic sugar over Promises — an
function always returns a Promise, and
pauses the function's execution until a Promise settles, then unwraps its value. The result is sequential-looking logic that composes naturally with standard control flow:
declaration (or expression) does two things: it ensures the function always returns a Promise, and it enables the use of
function and takes a Promise (or any thenable) as its operand — it suspends the function until that Promise resolves or rejects, then hands back the fulfilled value or throws the rejection reason.
does not block the JavaScript thread. The surrounding
function is suspended and control returns to the event loop, which remains free to process other tasks (UI events, other callbacks) while the awaited Promise is pending.
, chaining multiple asynchronous operations required deeply nested callbacks ("callback hell") or long
chains that were difficult to reason about, especially when conditional branches or loops were involved.
version expresses the same logic but uses ordinary
blocks that every JavaScript developer already knows. Branching, looping, and error handling no longer require special Promise-combinator knowledge.
Sequential async steps look sequential. Readers do not need to mentally unroll
boundaries in modern engines; stepping through in a debugger works intuitively.

the async function. Suspension means saving the function's local variable bindings and instruction pointer, then returning control to the event loop — the JavaScript thread is released and can run other code.
When the Promise settles, the event loop enqueues the continuation as a microtask. Microtasks run before the next I/O callback or timer, so the function resumes quickly. If the Promise fulfilled,
evaluates to the fulfillment value and execution continues. If it rejected,
re-throws the rejection reason, which can be caught by a surrounding
statement of an async function implicitly wraps the returned value in
. If the function throws, the returned Promise rejects with the thrown value.
The diagram below traces the execution of an

function call across the caller, the function itself, and the event loop. Note how the caller receives a pending Promise immediately, and the function only resumes once the event loop has fulfilled the inner Promise.
Sequential async steps depend on each other's results (fetch user → fetch that user's posts → enrich posts).

A React component that fetches a paginated list of products, cancels in-flight requests when the component unmounts or the page changes, and handles both network and HTTP errors:
Use AbortController for fetch requests in effects.

so stale requests do not update unmounted components.
If two fetches do not depend on each other, start both immediately and await the pair.
Always either handle the error meaningfully or re-throw it.

to cancel in-flight requests when a component unmounts, a user navigates away, or a new request supersedes an old one. Without cancellation, a slow response can arrive after a faster one and overwrite newer data (a race condition).
When user-supplied data is sent in a
header rather than concatenating strings — concatenation opens injection paths if the server does not parse JSON strictly.
For production systems, implement retry logic with exponential back-off (and a jitter to prevent thundering-herd problems), set request timeouts via
, and surface errors to the user rather than swallowing them.

function as a regular function that can pause mid-execution and hand its coffee mug to a coworker (the event loop) while it waits for something. The event loop goes off and handles other tasks; when the thing being waited for finishes, the coworker taps the function on the shoulder and hands back the result. From inside the function it feels like time stood still — the very next line has the value. From outside (the caller), all they see is a Promise that will eventually resolve.
does not freeze the thread — it only freezes
execution. Everything else in the JavaScript environment (UI events, other callbacks, timers) keeps running while the function waits.
suspends the function until a Promise settles, then unwraps its value (or rethrows on rejection).
does not block the JavaScript thread — it releases the event loop to handle other work while the awaited Promise is pending.
